source.title: Protein synthesis during sleep consolidates cortical plasticity in vivo

confidence_rationale: Strong causal evidence that mTOR-dependent protein synthesis during sleep (not wakefulness) is required to consolidate experience-dependent plasticity in visual cortex. Inhibiting mTOR during post-MD sleep abolishes ODP consolidation; the same inhibition during wakefulness has no effect on plasticity induction. Direct evidence that sleep consolidation is mechanistically distinct from waking plasticity induction -- wakefulness alone is insufficient for full consolidation.
failure_signatures: Rapamycin has broad effects on cellular protein synthesis; specificity to ODP-related proteins vs general cellular maintenance cannot be fully verified.

The study uses a short monocular deprivation paradigm (6 h MD) in cats at the critical period peak; whether the same sleep-specific consolidation requirement exists at other developmental stages is not tested.
The finding that waking mTOR inhibition does not impair induction does not necessarily mean waking is insufficient for consolidation under all conditions.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Intracortical inhibition of mTOR-dependent protein synthesis during post-MD sleep abolishes ODP consolidation in cat visual cortex, while the same inhibition during wakefulness has no effect on plasticity induction. Sleep also promotes phosphorylation of translation regulators (4E-BP1, eEF2) and translation (but not transcription) of ARC and BDNF mRNAs.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-120 claims that SWS-analog sleep performs consolidation work that cannot occur during active waking. The Seibt finding provides causal evidence for the 'waking is insufficient' premise: even with full plasticity induction during waking (the MD experience is registered), consolidation fails if the sleep translational program is blocked. The structural stabilisation that converts the update into a lasting change requires the offline phase. This supports MECH-120's claim that the SWS-analog offline phase is executing a specific consolidation program that waking cannot substitute for.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The protein synthesis requirement is for consolidation following an already-induced plasticity change. The REE monostrategy concern is whether sleep can rescue a substrate where waking dynamics have already produced excessive dominance -- this paper speaks to consolidation of waking-induced changes, not to reversal of maladaptive convergence. Additionally, the study uses a defined sensory deprivation paradigm and cats -- transfer to a self-generating policy convergence problem in a computational model requires additional assumptions.
mapping.source_context: Critical period cats (postnatal days 28-41); 6 h monocular deprivation then 8 h sleep; mTOR inhibitor rapamycin infused intracortically during post-MD sleep or during waking MD; ODP assessed with optical imaging and microelectrode recordings.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Current Biology, Frank lab, causal pharmacological intervention). Mapping fidelity moderate -- directly relevant to consolidation-requires-sleep claim but requires further step to reach the monostrategy rescue question. Transfer risk moderate.
